claims.claim_text: The opening paragraph presents a dated Azure outage anecdote with specific duration, costs, and revenue loss.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In November 2020, a SaaS company running their entire production workload on a single Azure VM in the East US region experienced an outage that lasted 9 hours. The VM's host server had a hardware failure. Because the company had not configured Availability Zones, had no VM Scale Set, and had no load balancer, their application was completely offline. Their customers, many of whom are running end-of-month financial reports, could not access the platform. The post-incident review revealed that the monthly cost to run their single D4s_v3 VM was $140. Adding a second VM in a different Availability Zone behind a Standard Load Balancer would have added $165/month. A $165/month insurance policy could have prevented a $420,000 revenue loss and a wave of customer churn.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A production workload running on a single Azure VM without zone redundancy, load balancing, or horizontal failover can go fully offline during an infrastructure failure, and the cost of basic high availability is often far lower than the cost of a prolonged outage.
claims.rationale: This is a fabricated-style incident narrative with precise dates, prices, and business-loss figures but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Azure offers hundreds of VM sizes.
claims.span_hint: section: Choosing the Right VM Size, first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Azure offers hundreds of VM sizes, organized into families based on the workload type they are optimized for.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Azure offers many VM sizes, organized into families based on the workload type they are optimized for.
claims.rationale: Microsoft documents a wide range of sizes and families, but this exact count claim is not anchored to a stable primary page.

claims.claim_text: B-series VMs accumulate CPU credits below baseline and spend them during bursts until they are throttled back to baseline.
claims.span_hint: section: The B-Series: Burstable VMs
claims.rationale: Microsoft's B-family documentation directly describes the CPU credit model and throttling behavior.

claims.claim_text: The module gives exact monthly prices for Standard_B2s and Standard_D2s_v5 and computes a 57% savings figure.
claims.span_hint: section: The B-Series: Burstable VMs, paragraph after diagram
claims.anchor_text: A Standard_B2s (2 vCPUs, 4 GB RAM) costs about $30/month, while an equivalent Standard_D2s_v5 costs about $70/month. For a dev/test VM that sits idle 80% of the time, B-series saves you 57%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For a lightly used dev/test VM, a burstable B-series instance can cost materially less than a comparable D-series VM, which is why B-series is often attractive for workloads that spend much of their time idle.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is sound, but the exact prices and percentage are unstable and region-dependent.

claims.claim_text: The CI/CD build-agent cost-reduction anecdote uses precise fleet size, utilization, prices, savings, and performance outcomes without a source.
claims.span_hint: section: The B-Series: Burstable VMs, bold War Story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: A team running 15 CI/CD build agents on D4s_v5 instances (4 vCPUs, 16 GB, ~$140/month each) was spending $2,100/month. They analyzed their build patterns and found that agents were busy only 25% of the time, with builds coming in bursts. Switching to B4ms instances (same specs, burstable) at ~$67/month each cut their compute bill to $1,005/month---a 52% reduction with no performance impact on build times.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Example pattern**: For workloads like build agents that spend much of their time idle and then burst briefly, moving from fixed-performance VMs to burstable VMs can reduce compute spend substantially if the credit model fits the workload.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed case study with precise operational and pricing numbers that cannot be honestly cited as written.

claims.claim_text: Availability Zones are physically separate locations within a region with independent power, cooling, and networking.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Zones (AZs), first paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's availability-zones overview directly states these isolation properties.

claims.claim_text: Azure availability zones support a 99.99% uptime SLA for virtual machines.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Zones (AZs), final sentence
claims.rationale: Microsoft Azure's availability-zones page explicitly markets the 99.99% VM uptime SLA.

claims.claim_text: Availability Sets place VMs across fault domains and update domains to reduce correlated failures and stagger maintenance.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Sets, first paragraph
claims.rationale: The availability-set overview directly defines fault domains, update domains, and their maintenance behavior.

claims.claim_text: Using Availability Sets with two or more VMs meets the 99.95% Azure SLA.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Sets, final sentence
claims.rationale: Microsoft's availability-options page explicitly ties availability sets to the 99.95% Azure SLA.

claims.claim_text: Managed Disks are encrypted at rest by default, support customer-managed keys, Azure Disk Encryption, and confidential disk encryption tied to the VM's TPM.
claims.span_hint: section: Disk Encryption
claims.rationale: Microsoft's managed-disk encryption overview directly documents default encryption-at-rest and the available encryption models.

claims.claim_text: VM Scale Sets are groups of load-balanced VMs that can automatically scale based on demand or schedule.
claims.span_hint: section: VM Scale Sets (VMSS): Horizontal Auto-Scaling, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's availability-options page describes VMSS as load-balanced VM groups that scale automatically.

claims.claim_text: Flexible orchestration mode is the recommended VMSS mode and supports mixed VM types.
claims.span_hint: section: Orchestration Modes, prose above table
claims.rationale: Microsoft's orchestration-modes documentation explicitly calls Flexible the recommended mode and describes mixed VM-type support.

claims.claim_text: Azure Compute Gallery provides versioning, replication, and sharing controls for custom images.
claims.span_hint: section: Custom Images with VMSS, second paragraph
claims.rationale: The Azure Compute Gallery overview directly lists versioning, regional replication, and sharing capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Azure Load Balancer is a Layer 4 service for TCP/UDP traffic that distributes traffic to healthy instances.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Load Balancer: Distributing Traffic, opening sentence
claims.rationale: Microsoft's reliability guidance directly defines Azure Load Balancer as a Layer 4 TCP/UDP service.

claims.claim_text: Azure Spot VMs can be up to 90% cheaper than pay-as-you-go VMs and can be evicted at any time.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Spot VMs, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Spot VM architecture guidance explicitly states the up-to-90% discount and eviction risk.

claims.claim_text: The module says Spot VM eviction can deallocate or hibernate the VM on eviction.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Spot VMs, Key Considerations bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - **Eviction policy**: You can choose to deallocate the VM or hibernate (for Windows VMs) upon eviction.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Eviction policy**: You can choose to deallocate or delete the VM when Azure evicts it.
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Spot VM docs describe deallocate and delete eviction policies, not hibernate-on-eviction.

claims.claim_text: Azure Reservations can reduce resource costs by up to 72% with one-year or three-year commitments, and the discount applies automatically to matching resources.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Reserved Virtual Machine Instances (RIs), opening paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's reservations documentation directly states the up-to-72% savings, one- and three-year terms, and automatic application to matching resources.

claims.claim_text: The first Did You Know item gives a precise host-maintenance cadence, pause duration, reboot exceptions, and Scheduled Events notice window.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1.  **Azure VMs have a "host maintenance" event roughly every 4-6 weeks** where Azure needs to update the physical host. For most VM sizes, Azure uses memory-preserving maintenance that pauses the VM for less than 30 seconds. But for some GPU and high-performance VM sizes, a full reboot is required. You can subscribe to Scheduled Events via the Instance Metadata Service to get 15 minutes of advance warning before maintenance begins.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1.  **Azure periodically performs host maintenance**. Many VM-impacting maintenance events are brief, and Scheduled Events can provide advance notice for many maintenance scenarios, but some VM families or update types can still require a reboot.
claims.rationale: Some parts of this are real, but the specific 4-6 week cadence bundles unsupported precision with otherwise supportable guidance.

claims.claim_text: The second Did You Know item gives an exact monthly price and 'cheapest VM Azure offers' superlative for Standard_B1ls.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 2
claims.anchor_text: 2.  **The Standard_B1ls VM (1 vCPU, 0.5 GB RAM) costs approximately $3.80 per month** and is the cheapest VM Azure offers. It is surprisingly useful for lightweight workloads like a bastion host, a DNS forwarder, or a small cron job runner. Many teams overlook it because 0.5 GB seems too small, but for a process that uses 100 MB of RAM, it is more than enough.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2.  **The Standard_B1ls is a very small, low-cost VM** that can still be useful for lightweight workloads like a bastion-style host, a small relay service, or a simple scheduled task runner.
claims.rationale: The exact price and 'cheapest' ranking are unstable marketing facts and should be removed rather than weakly anchored.

claims.claim_text: The third Did You Know item says Flexible orchestration can mix VM sizes in the same scale set since late 2023 and gives a specific Intel/AMD bursting example.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3.  **VM Scale Sets in Flexible orchestration mode can mix different VM sizes in the same scale set** since late 2023. This means you can have a baseline of Standard_D4s_v5 instances and burst with Standard_D4as_v5 (AMD) instances if Intel capacity is constrained. This is particularly useful during regional capacity shortages where a single VM size might not be available.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3.  **VM Scale Sets in Flexible orchestration mode can use instance mix to combine multiple VM sizes in one scale set**. This can improve provisioning success and cost flexibility when capacity is constrained.
claims.rationale: The current capability is real, but the rollout date and specific illustrative scenario shouldn't be left as asserted product history.

claims.claim_text: Deallocating a VM stops compute charges but attached disks continue to incur charges.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 4, first sentence
claims.rationale: Microsoft's billing-state documentation explicitly says deallocated VMs stop compute billing while resources like disks continue to incur charges.

claims.claim_text: The final Did You Know item uses exact Premium SSD pricing and fleet-level arithmetic to quantify residual costs for stopped VMs.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 4, pricing example
claims.anchor_text: A 128 GB Premium SSD costs about $19/month whether the VM is running or not. Teams that "save money" by stopping 50 VMs every night still pay $950/month for the disks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Even when compute is off, attached disks can still add up to a meaningful monthly bill across many stopped VMs.
claims.rationale: The billing principle is real, but the exact disk price and fleet-total math are unstable pricing examples.

further_reading.title: Availability Options for Azure Virtual Machines
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary overview for Availability Zones, Availability Sets, VM Scale Sets, and the core HA options discussed in the module.

further_reading.title: Orchestration Modes for Virtual Machine Scale Sets
further_reading.why_relevant: Best source for Flexible vs Uniform behavior, availability characteristics, and mixed-size support.

further_reading.title: Azure Managed Disk Types
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for current disk classes, performance envelopes, and workload-fit guidance.

further_reading.title: Azure Spot Virtual Machines
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical product documentation for Spot VM eviction behavior, notice timing, and operational tradeoffs.

notes: Excluded quiz prompts/answers, exercise verification text, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams per seed instructions. This pass focuses on prose assertions and the Did You Know callouts that most clearly need citation or rewrite handling.
